title: Randomised measurements of a disorder-induced entanglement transition in a neutral atom quantum processor
authors: Apollonas S. Matsoukas-Roubeas, Oscar Scholin, Lucas Sá, Arinjoy De, Majd Hamdan, Alexei Bylinskii, Andrew J. Daley, Dorian A. Gangloff
abstract: The development and spread of entanglement in complex quantum systems is central to exploring many-body phenomena out of equilibrium. Measuring entanglement dynamics can shed light on information scrambling and thermalisation, namely on transitions from many-body quantum chaos to localisation in disordered, interacting systems. In quantum computing systems, entanglement entropy and other nonlinear functions of the density matrix have been recently measured, in particular by using the randomised measurement toolbox. However, it is difficult to implement the required arbitrary unitary rotations on specific subsystems without universal local control. Here we devise and demonstrate the measurement of entanglement entropy in a programmable analogue quantum simulator using a randomised measurement protocol that leverages local energy tuning together with a global field to bypass the need for local gate control. We implement this on a commercially available neutral-atom quantum simulator, QuEra's Aquila, and use it to show how programmable disorder in the local Hamiltonian parameters leads to a transition from chaotic to localised entanglement dynamics. Given current decoherence times, we clearly resolve disorder-specific, time-dependent entanglement spreading in small systems. Our work extends the utility of programmable analogue quantum simulators, and opens further opportunities for wider randomised measurement toolboxes in a range of other analogue systems.
summary: This paper presents a new method for measuring entanglement in analog quantum simulators using only global controls and local energy tuning. Using the QuEra Aquila neutral-atom processor, the authors demonstrate that they can observe the transition from a quantum chaotic regime to a many-body localized regime. The protocol bypasses the need for local single-qubit gates by using a sequence of phase quenches. The results show distinct entanglement growth patterns (linear vs. logarithmic) that match numerical predictions even in the presence of noise.
main_problem: Measuring entanglement dynamics and the transition from quantum chaos to many-body localization (MBL) in analog quantum simulators that lack universal local gate control.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a randomized measurement protocol using global controls and local detuning to extract Rényi entropy, successfully resolving the transition from linear to logarithmic entanglement growth in a neutral-atom processor.
method: A modified randomized measurement toolbox using a phi-quench protocol (switching between non-commuting Hamiltonians) to approximate local rotations, combined with numerical simulations to account for decoherence and readout errors.
model_or_system: A 1D chain of Rydberg atoms (N=6 to N=12) in the QuEra Aquila platform, governed by a long-range interacting transverse-field Ising model with programmable local and global detuning.
key_observables: Second-order Rényi entropy (S2,A), von Neumann entropy (S1,A), subsystem purity, level spacing ratio (r-parameter), and Spectral Form Factor (SFF).
important_parameters: Rabi frequency (Omega), local detuning (Delta_local), global detuning (Delta_global), and interaction strength (J).
main_assumptions: The system is small-scale (N=6) to stay within coherence times; the protocol assumes the phi-quench sequence effectively approximates a 2-design.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of the atom array and Hamiltonian parameters. Fig 2: Validation of the randomized rotation protocol via Bloch vector trajectories. Fig 3: Experimental entanglement growth across low, moderate, and high disorder regimes. Fig 4: Numerical signatures of the transition (level statistics and SFF).
paper_structure: Introduction to entanglement dynamics and MBL; description of the hardware and Hamiltonian; development of the global-to-local randomized measurement protocol; experimental implementation on Aquila; results showing the disorder-induced transition; and error analysis/numerical validation.
why_interesting: It provides a practical way to perform complex many-body measurements (like Rényi entropy) on analog hardware that lacks the high-fidelity local gates required by standard randomized measurement toolboxes, bridging the gap between analog simulation and quantum information protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

In all cases, we take N = 12 qubits with 1000 disordered realisations, and ⟨∆i⟩= 0.5J, J = 5.42µs−1 nearest neighbor interac- tion strength, and Rabi drive Ω= 2.92J. FIG. 5. The parameter ⟨∆i⟩induces an overall shift of the many-body spectrum. a For low values, e.g., ⟨∆i⟩= 0.5J, the experimentally accessible initial state—whose energy is zero—lies approximately in the middle of the spectrum. b For large values, e.g., ⟨∆i⟩= 15J, the same initial state is located far from the spectral center, effectively outside the main support of the many-body density of states. We use 100 bins.

FIG. 7. Density plot of the von Neumann entropy of eigen- states for 1000 realisations of a N = 12 qubit system with ⟨∆i⟩= 0.5J, for an equal bipartition, as a function of the cor- responding eigenenergy, S1,A(En). In a, we set ∆local = −10J and in b we set ∆local = −0.5J. The color scale represents log10(count + 1) obtained from a 120 × 120 two-dimensional histogram, so brighter regions correspond to a higher density of eigenstates at a given energy-entropy pair.

FIG. 9. Example task submitted to Aquila showing the time-dependent functions Ω, ϕ, ∆local, ∆global. First ∆local is ramped while Ωis kept at 0 in order to freeze any spuri- ous excitations that can occur when ramping together with ∆local, then Ωis ramped up. The system remains constant for a time tevol. ∆local and ∆global are ramped to their de- sired values during the randomised phase. ϕ flips between 0 and π/2 with equal probability for, in the full experiment, 16 times (in this illustration, 12). Ωis then ramped down to 0 with ∆local still constant. Finally, ∆local and ∆global are also ramped down to 0.
